answer:
Iqbal's poem "Insaan" ("The Human Being"), a brief lyrical reflection, portrays humanity as a dynamic, striving entity amid a passive natural world.[^1] Contrasting the inert narcissus flower (a mere "compelled spectator") and the longing-less pine tree with the human "particle" aflame with power and yearning for vastness, Iqbal elevates the human as inherently wise, perceptive, and potent—capable, if it chooses, of reshaping reality itself ("change the very form of the garden").[^2] This vision emphasizes self-realization, agency, and an expansive inner force, aligning with Iqbal's broader philosophy of human potential as a microcosm of divine creativity.

Cynewulf's reflection on humanity in *Elene*'s epilogue (the poet's personal runic signature and closing meditation) takes a starkly contrasting eschatological tone.[^3] Here, the aging poet laments human transience ("Joy has waned with the years, youth is gone"), frailty ("stained by my deeds, shackled by sins"), and subjection to worldly flux, culminating in the Last Judgment where God divides every person into three tiers amid purifying fire: the righteous (who endure it easily), the sinful (tormented in the middle), and the accursed (cast into hell's abyss).[^4] Human identity is thus defined by inevitable decay, moral failing, and utter dependence on Christ's mercy for refinement and eternal inheritance— a communal, sin-bound soul awaiting divine verdict.

**Core difference**: Iqbal's humanistic optimism celebrates the human as an active, world-altering force with innate godlike potency, free from passive nature's limits. Cynewulf's Christian pessimism frames humanity as fleeting, sinful, and passive before God's judgment, redeemable only through faith and grace. Iqbal empowers individual striving in this life; Cynewulf subordinates it to eternal accountability.[^5]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch.12 "Insaan" [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/12-insaan/translation/#p-e60b77).
[^2]: Ibid., [paragraphs](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/12-insaan/translation/#p-cb3c43) (striving power), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/12-insaan/translation/#p-587d8c) (agency over creation).
[^3]: Cynewulf, *Elene*, ch.1 [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-d2e607).
[^4]: Ibid., [paragraphs](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-8f8a1b) (transience), [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-dcbbfe) (threefold judgment).
[^5]: For side-by-side structural comparison on human themes, see tool analysis in *compare_works* (Bang-E-Dara Part 3 ch.12 nearest to Cynewulf's *Elene* ch.1 via TF-IDF: 0.19 cosine).
